schools.summary: Gill: Jacob, after he had passed over the brook and was come to his wives and children, lifted up his eyes, either accidentally or on purpose, to see if he could espy his brother coming; the witnesses note Jacob looking up and seeing Esau coming with four hundred men, and dividing the children among Leah, Rachel, and the two handmaids — the dreaded moment arrived, Jacob ordering his household for the meeting (the long-feared encounter at hand: 'Jacob lifted up his eyes, and looked, and, behold, Esau came, and with him four hundred men. And he divided the children unto Leah, and unto Rachel, and unto the two handmaids'; the moment of truth, that Jacob, having wrestled and prevailed in the night, now lifts his eyes and sees the very sight he had dreaded, 'Esau... and with him four hundred men,' the armed band advancing; Jacob's composure and care even now, that he does not flee but 'divided the children unto Leah, and unto Rachel, and unto the two handmaids,' arranging his household in order for the meeting; that this dividing was by their mothers, setting each group under its own mother for the careful and orderly approach, so Jacob, seeing Esau come, marshals his family to meet him).

themes: behold, Esau came, and with him four hundred men
And he divided the children unto Leah, and unto Rachel, and unto the two handmaids
the dreaded moment arrived, Jacob ordering his household
composure after the night of prevailing at Peniel

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Jacob put the handmaids and their children foremost, Leah and her children after, and Rachel and Joseph last; Gill: the handmaids and their children were placed first, in the first division, as being less honourable and less beloved by him; the witnesses note Jacob placing the handmaids and their children first, Leah and hers next, and Rachel and Joseph last — the careful arrangement that put the most beloved in the safest place (Jacob ranging his family in an order that reveals his heart: 'the handmaids and their children foremost, and Leah and her children after, and Rachel and Joseph hindmost'; the principle of the arrangement, that Jacob set them in rising order of his love and esteem, the handmaids, Bilhah and Zilpah, and their children 'foremost,' first to meet Esau and so most exposed if there were danger, then Leah and hers, and 'Rachel and Joseph hindmost,' last and so safest, the most beloved wife and son set farthest from peril; the tenderness and the prudence in this, that Jacob, while honouring all, placed those dearest to him where they would be least in danger should Esau prove hostile; that this ordering, though it shows his deeper love for Rachel and Joseph, was a father's care to shield the most beloved, so Jacob arranges his family by degrees of affection, the dearest hindmost and safest).

themes: he put the handmaids and their children foremost
and Rachel and Joseph hindmost
Jacob ranging his family in rising order of love
a father's care to shield the most beloved from peril

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob passed over at the head of them, as the master of the family, exposing himself to the greatest danger for them, in order to protect and defend them in the best manner he could, or to endeavour to soften the mind of his brother; the witnesses note Jacob passing over before his family and bowing himself seven times to the ground until he came near Esau — the brave and humble approach, Jacob placing himself foremost and doing deep homage to his brother (Jacob not hiding behind his household but going first to meet the danger: 'he passed over before them, and bowed himself to the ground seven times, until he came near to his brother'; two graces in this act, first his courage and self-sacrifice, that Jacob 'passed over before them,' putting himself at the head, foremost and most exposed, to shield his family and be the first to meet Esau, and second his deep humility, that he 'bowed himself to the ground seven times' as he drew near, the lowliest oriental homage, a sevenfold reverence by which he abased himself before his brother to disarm his anger and win his favour; that Jacob, the prince who had power with God, now humbles himself to the very ground before Esau, mingling courage with the deepest submission, so Jacob goes foremost and bows seven times before his brother).

themes: he passed over before them
and bowed himself to the ground seven times, until he came near to his brother
Jacob going foremost and bowing in deep homage
courage and self-sacrifice joined to the deepest humility

schools.summary: Gill: Esau, if he rode on any creature (which is likely), alighted from it on sight of his brother Jacob, and to express his joy on that occasion and affection for him made all the haste he could to meet him, as did the father of the prodigal; the witnesses note Esau running to meet Jacob, embracing him, falling on his neck, kissing him, and both weeping — the overwhelming reconciliation, Esau's heart turned to love, God's answer to Jacob's prayer (the dreaded meeting dissolving in tears of joy: 'Esau ran to meet him, and embraced him, and fell on his neck, and kissed him: and they wept'; the wonder of the change, that the brother who came with four hundred men, whom Jacob feared would smite him, now 'ran to meet him' in eager affection, 'embraced him,' 'fell on his neck,' 'kissed him,' and the two brothers 'wept' together; that this is God's own answer to Jacob's prayer of the night, the LORD having turned Esau's heart from vengeance to love, melting his anger into tears; the likeness, as Gill observes, to the father of the prodigal, running, falling on the neck, and kissing; that, though some have suspected Esau's kiss, the commentators here take it as a genuine and God-wrought reconciliation, so the long enmity ends in a brother's embrace and tears).

sides.position: A genuine, God-wrought reconciliation — Esau's heart truly turned from vengeance to love, in answer to Jacob's prayer
Some (Jewish tradition, noting the dotted text) have suspected the kiss; the commentators here do not adopt this

themes: Esau ran to meet him, and embraced him, and fell on his neck, and kissed him
and they wept
the overwhelming reconciliation of the brothers
God's answer to Jacob's prayer, turning Esau's heart to love

schools.summary: Gill: after the salutation had passed between him and his brother Jacob, Esau looked and saw behind him the women and children, Jacob's two wives and his two handmaids and twelve children; the witnesses note Esau asking who the women and children were, and Jacob answering that they were the children God had graciously given him — the gracious confession that ascribed his family to God's free gift (Esau, his anger gone, turning with kindly interest to Jacob's household: 'Who are those with thee? And he said, The children which God hath graciously given thy servant'; the beauty of Jacob's answer, that, asked about his children, he does not boast of them as his own achievement but ascribes them wholly to God, 'the children which God hath graciously given thy servant'; the two graces in the reply, the owning of God as the giver, 'God hath graciously given,' that his children were God's free and gracious gift not his own getting, and the continued humility, 'thy servant,' Jacob still using the lowly title before Esau; that Jacob seizes even this question to give glory to God, confessing his family as undeserved mercy, so Jacob ascribes his children to the gracious gift of God).

themes: Who are those with thee?
The children which God hath graciously given thy servant
Jacob ascribing his children wholly to God's grace
owning God as the giver, keeping his lowly title

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the handmaidens came near, they and their children, and bowed themselves; Gill: these were Bilhah and her two sons Dan and Naphtali, and Zilpah and her two sons Gad and Asher, being foremost and next to Jacob; the witnesses note the handmaids and their children coming near and bowing themselves to Esau — the first company doing reverence, the orderly presentation of the family begun (the family now coming forward in the order Jacob had set, the foremost first: 'the handmaidens came near, they and their children, and they bowed themselves'; the orderly approach, that the handmaids, Bilhah and Zilpah, and their four sons, Dan, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher, being placed foremost, 'came near' first and 'bowed themselves' before Esau in respect; that the whole family followed Jacob's own example of humble reverence, each company in turn coming near and bowing to Esau as to a lord; the harmony of the household in this submission, all uniting in the homage Jacob had begun, that the reconciliation might be complete and the honour to Esau full, so the handmaids and their children come near and bow before Esau).

themes: the handmaidens came near, they and their children
and they bowed themselves
the first company doing reverence to Esau
the family following Jacob's example of humble reverence

schools.summary: Gill: Leah's children were seven, Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun, and Dinah, six sons and one daughter; they were in the next division or company; the witnesses note Leah and her children bowing next, and last Joseph and Rachel coming near and bowing — the whole family in turn doing homage, the most beloved presented last (the presentation completed by the remaining companies: 'Leah also with her children came near, and bowed themselves: and after came Joseph near and Rachel, and they bowed themselves'; the order kept to the end, that after the handmaids 'Leah also with her children,' her seven, six sons and Dinah, 'came near, and bowed themselves,' and last of all 'Joseph near and Rachel,' the most beloved, hindmost and safest, came and 'bowed themselves' before Esau; the touching detail that Joseph is named before Rachel, 'Joseph near and Rachel,' some seeing in it that the boy went before his mother, perhaps to shield her, a small mark of his forwardness even then; that the whole family, from the least honoured to the most beloved, united in the same humble reverence to Esau, so all the household, Rachel and Joseph last, bow before Esau in turn).

themes: Leah also with her children came near, and bowed themselves
and after came Joseph near and Rachel, and they bowed themselves
the whole family doing homage to Esau in turn
the most beloved, Joseph and Rachel, presented last and safest

schools.summary: Gill: Esau asked not as being ignorant of the design of it, for no doubt the several drovers, according to their instructions from Jacob, had acquainted him with it, but he chose not to take the present without showing some reluctance; the witnesses note Esau asking what Jacob meant by the droves he met, and Jacob answering they were to find grace in his sight — the courteous exchange over the gift, Jacob owning his purpose to win his brother's favour (Esau, having met the droves on the way, now asking their meaning: 'What meanest thou by all this drove which I met?'; that Esau's question was not from ignorance, for the drovers had already told him whose the gift was and why, but, as Gill notes, Esau out of courtesy and a show of reluctance would have Jacob himself declare it, perhaps meaning to decline so large a present; Jacob's plain and humble answer, 'These are to find grace in the sight of my lord,' owning openly that the gift was sent to win Esau's favour and seal their peace; the candour of Jacob, hiding nothing of his purpose, that the present was an offering of goodwill to his brother, so Jacob declares the droves were sent to find grace with Esau).

themes: What meanest thou by all this drove which I met?
These are to find grace in the sight of my lord
the courteous exchange over the present's purpose
Jacob owning plainly the gift was to win Esau's favour

schools.summary: Gill: Esau said, 'I have much,' and stood in no need of this present, or had much more than Jacob, and so would have Jacob keep that he had unto himself; the witnesses note Esau saying he had enough and bidding Jacob keep his gift — the generous refusal of a softened brother, content with his own (Esau, his heart full, at first declining the present: 'I have enough, my brother; keep that thou hast unto thyself'; the change in Esau's spirit, that the once-vengeful brother now speaks with warmth and generosity, calling Jacob 'my brother,' the word of restored kinship, and declining the gift with 'I have enough,' literally 'I have much,' content with his own and not wishing to take from Jacob; the contrast often drawn, that the worldly Esau says 'I have enough' while Jacob says God has dealt graciously and given him all, both content, but Esau with his much and Jacob with God's grace; that Esau's refusal sprang not from coldness but from a brotherly generosity, unwilling to be enriched at Jacob's cost, so Esau, content and softened, would have Jacob keep his own).

themes: I have enough, my brother
keep that thou hast unto thyself
Esau's generous declining of the gift
the once-vengeful brother now warm and content

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: Jacob could not be certain that he had found favour with Esau unless the present had been received, for the receiving of it was the proof of reconciliation; Gill notes Jacob's 'nay, I pray thee,' do not refuse my present; the witnesses note Jacob pressing Esau to receive the gift, saying he had seen his face as the face of God, who was pleased with him — the earnest urging of the present, the gift the very proof of reconciliation, Esau's kindness likened to God's favour (Jacob not to be refused, but pressing the gift with a remarkable plea: 'if now I have found grace in thy sight, then receive my present at my hand: for therefore I have seen thy face, as though I had seen the face of God, and thou wast pleased with me'; Jacob's reasoning, that the acceptance of the gift would be the proof and seal of reconciliation as Clarke notes, so Jacob urges Esau to receive it as the token that grace was indeed found; the striking comparison, 'I have seen thy face, as though I had seen the face of God,' that Jacob, fresh from Peniel where he saw 'the face of God' and lived, sees in Esau's softened, gracious face a reflection of God's own favour, the reconciled brother's countenance mirroring the reconciled God's; that, just as God had been pleased with Jacob at Peniel, so Esau 'wast pleased with me,' so Jacob urges the gift, seeing God's favour shining in his brother's face).

themes: receive my present at my hand
for therefore I have seen thy face, as though I had seen the face of God
Jacob urging the gift as the seal of reconciliation
Esau's softened face mirroring the favour met at Peniel

schools.summary: Gill: the present he had sent him, now carrying home to his house, was a part of what God had blessed Jacob with, which from a beneficent generous spirit he gave his brother, wishing the blessing of God to go with it; the witnesses note Jacob calling the gift his blessing, urging Esau because God had dealt graciously and he had enough, and Esau taking it — the gift pressed as a blessing, Jacob content in God's abundant grace (Jacob crowning his urging with the deepest reason: 'Take, I pray thee, my blessing that is brought to thee; because God hath dealt graciously with me, and because I have enough. And he urged him, and he took it'; the rich naming of the gift, that Jacob calls it 'my blessing,' a portion of the blessing God had given him, freely passed on to his brother with a wish of God's favour; the ground of Jacob's generosity, 'because God hath dealt graciously with me, and because I have enough,' that Jacob can give freely because God has given him all; the contrast with Esau being complete, that Esau said 'I have much' but Jacob says 'I have enough' because 'God hath dealt graciously,' resting his sufficiency in God's grace not in mere abundance; that Jacob 'urged him, and he took it,' the gift at last accepted, the reconciliation sealed, so Jacob gives his blessing content in grace, and Esau receives it).

debates.question: What is the difference between Esau's 'I have much' and Jacob's 'I have enough'?

sides.position: Esau rests his sufficiency in mere abundance ('I have much'); Jacob rests his in God's grace — 'I have enough' because 'God hath dealt graciously with me'

themes: Take, I pray thee, my blessing that is brought to thee
because God hath dealt graciously with me, and because I have enough
the gift pressed as a blessing, content in God's grace
Esau accepting it, the reconciliation sealed

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Esau said, 'Let us take our journey, and let us go, and I will go before thee,' offering to accompany Jacob; Gill: Esau invited Jacob to go with him to Seir, where he lived, to stop a while and refresh himself and his family; the witnesses note Esau proposing that they journey together, he going before Jacob — the brotherly offer to accompany and lead, springing from Esau's restored goodwill (Esau, the reconciliation now full, wishing the brothers to travel on together: 'Let us take our journey, and let us go, and I will go before thee'; the warmth of the offer, that Esau, his heart wholly won, proposes that they 'take our journey... together' and that he himself 'go before thee,' leading the way and perhaps acting as Jacob's guide and protector toward Seir where he dwelt; the genuine kindness in this, that Esau wished to keep his recovered brother by him, to honour and escort him; that the offer, though kindly meant, raised a difficulty for Jacob, whose tender flocks and children could not keep pace with Esau's swift band and whose path lay elsewhere, so Esau, in brotherly love, offers to journey on with Jacob and lead the way).

themes: Let us take our journey, and let us go
and I will go before thee
Esau's brotherly offer to travel together and lead
his heart wholly won, wishing to keep his brother by him

schools.summary: Gill: the eldest child being but thirteen years of age and the youngest about six, Esau might easily perceive by their stature that they were young and tender, and not able to bear either riding or walking very hard; the witnesses note Jacob explaining that the children were tender and the flocks with young, so that overdriving one day would kill the flock — the prudent and considerate reason for declining Esau's pace (Jacob, with respectful care, declining to keep Esau's swift pace: 'My lord knoweth that the children are tender, and the flocks and herds with young are with me: and if men should overdrive them one day, all the flock will die'; the wisdom and tenderness of the plea, that Jacob points to 'the children,' young and tender, the eldest but thirteen and the youngest about six, and 'the flocks and herds with young,' the nursing and breeding cattle, all of which would suffer or perish if driven hard; the shepherd's care in the warning, 'if men should overdrive them one day, all the flock will die,' a gentle and reasonable ground for travelling at his own slower pace; that Jacob, while declining the escort, does so with all courtesy, calling Esau 'my lord' and appealing to his understanding, so Jacob gives the tender flocks and children as his reason for a slower journey).

themes: My lord knoweth that the children are tender
if men should overdrive them one day, all the flock will die
Jacob declining Esau's swift pace with a shepherd's care
the tender children and nursing flocks his courteous reason

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob desired in a very respectful manner that Esau would not keep his pace in complaisance to him, but proceed on in his journey and go on with his men, and he with his family and flocks would follow after as fast as he could; the witnesses note Jacob asking Esau to go before while he leads on softly as the cattle and children can bear, until he comes to Seir — the courteous declining of the escort, Jacob to follow at his own gentle pace (Jacob proposing the solution: 'Let my lord, I pray thee, pass over before his servant: and I will lead on softly, according as the cattle... and the children be able to endure, until I come unto my lord unto Seir'; the courtesy and prudence, that Jacob begs Esau to 'pass over before,' go on ahead at his own swift pace not waiting for Jacob, while Jacob himself would 'lead on softly,' travel gently at the pace the cattle and children could bear; the difficulty some have raised, that Jacob says 'until I come unto my lord unto Seir' yet, as the next verses show, he went not to Seir but to Succoth and Shechem, some taking this as Jacob's prudent caution, not wishing to bind himself or travel with Esau's band, others as a courteous intention later altered by circumstance or God's leading; that, whatever the case, Jacob wisely sought to part from Esau in peace and go his own way, so Jacob asks Esau to go ahead while he follows softly).

debates.question: Why did Jacob say he would come to Seir, when he went instead to Succoth and Shechem?

sides.position: Prudent caution — Jacob, not wishing to travel with or be bound to Esau's band, courteously parted, intending to go his own way
A genuine, courteous intention later altered by circumstance or God's leading (he may have meant to visit Seir at another time)

themes: Let my lord, I pray thee, pass over before his servant
I will lead on softly... until I come unto my lord unto Seir
Jacob's courteous declining of the escort
leading on at a gentle pace, seeking to part in peace

schools.summary: John Wesley: Esau offers some of his men to be his guard and convoy, but Jacob humbly declines it; Gill: Esau offered to leave some of his folk to show Jacob the way and guard him on the road, and that he might appear the more honourable when he entered into Seir; the witnesses note Esau offering to leave some of his men with Jacob, and Jacob declining, asking only to find grace in his sight — the kindly offer of an escort gently refused, Jacob desiring only goodwill (Esau, still wishing to honour and protect his brother, making a further offer: 'Let me now leave with thee some of the folk that are with me'; the continued kindness of Esau, that, having failed to keep Jacob with him, he would at least 'leave... some of the folk,' some of his four hundred men, as a guard and guide for Jacob's road and an honour to him; Jacob's gracious refusal, 'What needeth it?' that Jacob, not wishing to be beholden to Esau's men nor to travel under their watch, courteously declines, asking only the one thing he truly desired, 'let me find grace in the sight of my lord'; that Jacob sought from Esau not an escort but his favour, content with the reconciliation and the goodwill restored, so Jacob declines the escort desiring only his brother's grace).

themes: Let me now leave with thee some of the folk that are with me
What needeth it? let me find grace in the sight of my lord
Esau's kindly offer of an escort gently declined
Jacob desiring not a guard but only his brother's favour

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: here Jacob and Esau part friends; Esau is loving and Jacob wary, and the reconciliation, once made, is not broken; Gill: Esau took his leave of Jacob the same day he met him and proceeded on in his journey toward Seir (whether he arrived the same day is uncertain, probably it was more than a day's journey); the witnesses note Esau returning that day on his way to Seir — the peaceful parting of the brothers, each going his own way in restored goodwill (the meeting ending as it should, in a friendly parting: 'Esau returned that day on his way unto Seir'; the quiet close of the great reconciliation, that Esau, his errand of love accomplished and his offers kindly declined, 'returned that day' toward his own country, 'unto Seir,' leaving Jacob to follow his own path; that, as Matthew Henry notes, 'Jacob and Esau part friends,' the long enmity healed, Esau loving and Jacob wary, but the peace between them real and lasting; the providence of God in the whole, that the dreaded meeting Jacob had feared would be his destruction ended in embrace, gift, and a peaceful parting, every fear answered by grace, so Esau returns to Seir and the brothers part in peace).

themes: So Esau returned that day on his way unto Seir
the peaceful parting of the brothers
the long enmity healed, Jacob and Esau parting friends
the dreaded meeting ending in embrace, gift, and peace

schools.summary: Gill: Succoth was on the other side of Jordan, so called by anticipation, for it had its name from what follows, as yet there was no city built here (or at least of this name); Jacob came here perhaps after he had been at Seir and stayed a little; the witnesses note Jacob journeying to Succoth, building a house and booths for his cattle, whence the place was named Succoth — the resting-place named for the booths Jacob made, his first settled halt after the meeting (Jacob, parted from Esau, moving to a place of rest and giving it its name: 'Jacob journeyed to Succoth, and built him an house, and made booths for his cattle: therefore the name of the place is called Succoth'; the settling of Jacob, that, after the long journey and the dreaded meeting now past, Jacob comes to a place where he 'built him an house,' a more permanent dwelling, 'and made booths for his cattle,' shelters or folds for his beasts, tokens of a longer stay; the origin of the name 'Succoth,' which means 'booths,' the place taking its name from the booths Jacob raised there; that the name was used 'by anticipation,' for as yet there was no city, only the booths Jacob made, which gave the spot its lasting name, so Jacob settles at Succoth, named for the booths he built).

themes: Jacob journeyed to Succoth, and built him an house, and made booths for his cattle
therefore the name of the place is called Succoth
Jacob's settled resting-place after the meeting
the place named 'booths' from the shelters he raised

schools.summary: Gill: this was not Salem, of which Melchizedek was king, much less Jerusalem, for it was forty miles from it, but more likely the city of Shechem, or Salim near Aenon; the word 'Shalem' may be read either as a name or 'in peace'/'whole'; the witnesses note Jacob coming to Shalem, a city of Shechem in Canaan, from Padan-aram, and pitching his tent before the city — the safe arrival in the land of promise, Jacob come whole and in peace from his long exile (Jacob at last reaching Canaan: 'Jacob came to Shalem, a city of Shechem, which is in the land of Canaan, when he came from Padanaram; and pitched his tent before the city'; the great mercy in this arrival, that Jacob, who twenty years before had fled from Canaan a lone fugitive, now 'came... in the land of Canaan,' safe back in the land of promise, 'when he came from Padanaram'; the reading of 'Shalem,' some taking it as the name of a place, a city of Shechem, others rendering it 'in peace' or 'whole,' that Jacob came 'safe' and 'in peace' to Shechem, his body whole the limp notwithstanding, his family entire, his goods preserved; that Jacob 'pitched his tent before the city,' settling near Shechem in the land God had promised, so Jacob comes safe and in peace to Canaan and pitches by Shechem).

sides.position: The name of a place — Shalem, a city of (or near) Shechem
'In peace' or 'whole' — that Jacob came safe and entire to Shechem from Padan-aram

themes: Jacob came to Shalem, a city of Shechem, which is in the land of Canaan
and pitched his tent before the city
Jacob's safe arrival in the land of promise
come whole and in peace from his long exile

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Jacob bought the parcel of field where he had spread his tent from the children of Hamor for a hundred pieces of money; Gill: Jacob bought a part, not the whole, of the field, though he was heir of the whole country, because as yet the time was not come for him or his to take possession of it; the witnesses note Jacob buying a parcel of the field where his tent was spread from the sons of Hamor for a hundred pieces of money — the lawful purchase of a foothold in the promised land, the patriarch buying what was already his by promise (Jacob securing a lawful title to the ground: 'he bought a parcel of a field, where he had spread his tent, at the hand of the children of Hamor, Shechem's father, for an hundred pieces of money'; the significance of this purchase, that Jacob 'bought a parcel,' a portion not the whole, of the field where his tent stood, paying 'an hundred pieces of money' to 'the children of Hamor,' the lawful owners; the irony and the faith in it, that Jacob, who was by God's promise heir of all Canaan, yet buys a small parcel with money because, as Gill notes, the time for his seed to possess the land was not yet come, and he would hold even this foothold by honest purchase; that this parcel became a lasting possession, where Joseph's bones were later laid, so Jacob buys a foothold in the land that was his by promise).

themes: he bought a parcel of a field, where he had spread his tent
at the hand of the children of Hamor, Shechem's father, for an hundred pieces of money
the lawful purchase of a foothold in the promised land
heir of all Canaan, yet buying a small parcel by honest purchase

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob erected there an altar to offer sacrifice upon to God, by way of thanksgiving for the many mercies he had received since he went out of the land of Canaan, and especially for his safety in journeying hither from Padan-aram and his safe return; he called it El-elohe-Israel, that is, 'God, the God of Israel'; the witnesses note Jacob erecting an altar and calling it El-elohe-Israel — the altar of thanksgiving that confessed the God who had kept him, sealed with his own new name (Jacob, safely home, crowning his arrival with worship: 'he erected there an altar, and called it Elelohe-Israel'; the act and the name, that Jacob 'erected... an altar' to offer sacrifice to God in thanksgiving for all the mercies of his long journey, above all for his safe return to Canaan, and he 'called it Elelohe-Israel,' which means 'God, the God of Israel'; the rich confession in the name, that Jacob owns God as his own God under his own new name 'Israel,' the prince who prevailed at Peniel, the God who had been the God of Abraham and Isaac now confessed as 'the God of Israel,' his God by covenant and by the night's wrestling; that this altar marks Jacob's grateful return to the worship of God in the promised land, sealing his pilgrimage with praise, so Jacob raises an altar to the God of Israel in thanksgiving for his keeping).

themes: he erected there an altar
and called it Elelohe-Israel
the altar of thanksgiving to 'God, the God of Israel'
owning God as his own under his new name Israel
